author: role.analyst.cooperative-systems

dimensionSummaries.dimension.player-fantasy: Each participant is one member of a duplicated hero team that must coordinate to advance while still competing to be the most successful collector.
dimensionSummaries.dimension.core-loop: Two to four players enter a themed area, split up or regroup, exchange access to tools, solve simultaneous-action obstacles, collect rupees, defeat a boss, and compare individual results.
dimensionSummaries.dimension.verbs: Top-down swordplay, carrying or throwing teammates, pulling and pushing together, using a currently held tool, separating, regrouping, and opportunistic collection make social coordination part of the action set.
dimensionSummaries.dimension.world-state: Stage-local switches, defeated enemies, collected rupees, tool availability, and player positions form a shared synchronized state; major progress is organized by completed areas and earned keys.
dimensionSummaries.dimension.topology: Separate handheld screens allow players to occupy different nearby spaces, while choke points and multi-person mechanisms repeatedly draw the team back together.
dimensionSummaries.dimension.gating-revisit: Player count, synchronized presence, carried tools, rupee performance, area completion, and earned keys gate progress; replay supports higher collection performance rather than a solitary world revisit.
dimensionSummaries.dimension.puzzle-information: Obstacles signal required headcount, simultaneous timing, complementary positions, or a swappable tool; the solution often exists in communication between differently situated players.
dimensionSummaries.dimension.dungeon-structure: The adventure is a sequence of multiplayer dungeon areas with local challenge rooms, collection targets, and bosses rather than one continuous overworld-to-dungeon map.
dimensionSummaries.dimension.exploration-reward: Rupees reward route coverage and speed while hidden paths, tool opportunities, and team separation create a tension between collective clearance and individual score.
dimensionSummaries.dimension.growth-economy: Collected rupees act as both required progress currency and comparative score; most tools are situational stage resources rather than a growing permanent solo inventory.
dimensionSummaries.dimension.combat-boss: Top-down encounters scale around several independent attackers and can require coordinated positions or shared exploitation while allowing players to compete for nearby rewards.
dimensionSummaries.dimension.npc-quest: The social system is primarily player-to-player rather than NPC-driven; communication, assistance, obstruction, and score comparison create the responsive relationship layer.
dimensionSummaries.dimension.narrative-performance: A concise rescue frame establishes why the hero is divided, then stage starts, team reunions, boss clears, rupee tallies, and key awards provide the main performance cadence.
dimensionSummaries.dimension.camera-blocking: Each player has a centered personal handheld view, so blocking relies on local object readability and shared choke points rather than keeping every participant inside one camera.
dimensionSummaries.dimension.teaching-feedback: Simple top-down verbs, explicit player-count obstacles, immediate switch and object reactions, rupee totals, and peer demonstration let the group teach itself during play.
dimensionSummaries.dimension.ui-input: Every participant uses a Game Boy Advance with a private screen and compact controls; individual health, held tool, position, and collection state remain locally readable.
dimensionSummaries.dimension.recovery-accessibility: A mistake affects local health, position, or shared collection performance more often than erasing the whole session, but assembling the required hardware and players is a substantial access cost.
dimensionSummaries.dimension.perspective-implementation: Top-down personal views permit independent movement and off-screen separation while tile-like interaction zones make synchronized switches, carries, and enemy states precise.
dimensionSummaries.dimension.hardware-multiplayer: The original Four Swords requires two to four Game Boy Advance systems, a copy for each participant, and link cables; it has no original single-player mode.
dimensionSummaries.dimension.production-runtime: A faithful abstraction needs deterministic multi-client state, low-latency position and switch synchronization, player-count-aware dependencies, per-player cameras and UI, stage scoring, and disconnect-safe recovery.

observationThemes.context: Several players traverse the same stage while both a shared clearance goal and individual rupee totals remain active.
preconditions: Two to four linked participants are present.
A route contains both collective dependencies and collectible opportunities.
playerInput: Choose when to help, separate, carry a teammate, use a tool, or race toward a visible reward.
before: The team needs collective progress, but individual ranking is unsettled.
action: Perform a verb that can advance the group while changing personal position or score opportunity.
after: A shared obstacle may clear while collection advantage shifts between players.
feedback: Immediate object response, separate player movement, rupee pickup effects, and individual totals expose both outcomes.
consequences: Momentary self-interest creates conversation and improvisation without replacing the common objective.
observationThemes.factualDescription: The game requires players to work together through multiplayer dungeons while rupees contribute to individual comparison and overall advancement.
observationThemes.interpretation: Cooperation gains social texture when the same space offers low-stakes competitive temptations that do not invalidate the shared win condition.

observationThemes.context: Personal screens allow players to explore different branches until a multi-person obstacle requires reunion.
preconditions: The local topology has separable routes.
A downstream gate checks simultaneous presence or combined force.
playerInput: Split to inspect branches, communicate findings, then navigate back and synchronize at the gate.
before: Information and collectibles are distributed across more space than one group path efficiently covers.
action: Use independent cameras for parallel search and a headcount rule for reunion.
after: The team consolidates at the dependency and opens the shared onward route.
feedback: Personal viewport changes, peer communication, player markers, gate response, and reunion animation make group state legible.
consequences: Topology creates a rhythm of autonomy and coordination instead of requiring constant formation.
observationThemes.factualDescription: Each participant views and controls a separate hero, can move apart from the others, and encounters obstacles that require multiple heroes acting together.
observationThemes.interpretation: Alternating parallel branches with explicit reunion gates can exploit multiple viewpoints without losing a coherent team route.

observationThemes.context: A stage obstacle needs a particular item while players currently hold different situational tools.
preconditions: The required tool is available somewhere in the stage.
The team can change or exchange its tool distribution.
playerInput: Identify the matching affordance, locate the tool, and assign or transfer it to a suitably positioned player.
before: The group has enough people but the wrong capability distribution at the dependency.
action: Reconfigure who carries which local verb.
after: The obstacle resolves and the team's remaining loadout creates new tradeoffs.
feedback: Tool icon, pickup or swap response, environmental reaction, and opened path confirm the allocation.
consequences: Inventory becomes a communication problem and a reason for players to exchange roles within a stage.
observationThemes.factualDescription: Players use and swap items while solving multiplayer dungeon obstacles, and those tools are more situational than a single permanently expanding inventory.
observationThemes.interpretation: Restricting simultaneous capability per participant can turn a familiar item lock into a cooperative allocation decision.

observationThemes.context: A puzzle, reward cluster, or dangerous room makes one player's behavior immediately relevant to the others.
preconditions: All participants share the stage state.
Each has a private centered display and independent input.
playerInput: Signal verbally or through movement, then assist, race, wait, or playfully obstruct.
before: No authored character response can determine the social outcome.
action: Let human participants negotiate the local tactic.
after: The group's trust, temporary roles, and pace change even if the formal quest state only advances one step.
feedback: Live movement, object consequences, score change, laughter or verbal response, and the final tally expose social impact.
consequences: Replay variation comes primarily from people rather than additional scripted dialogue branches.
observationThemes.factualDescription: The original requires multiple human-controlled heroes, and its official description explicitly combines cooperation with competition over rupees.
observationThemes.interpretation: In a mandatory multiplayer work, other players can occupy design functions normally assigned to reactive NPCs, provided their effects are immediately visible.

observationThemes.context: One participant mistimes a combat or coordination action during a shared stage.
preconditions: The stage session and link connection remain active.
Other participants can continue acting or repeat the local interaction.
playerInput: Recover position or health, regroup, and retry the immediate dependency with peer guidance.
before: The team loses efficiency, resources, or a scoring opportunity.
action: Absorb the local penalty without dissolving the multiplayer session.
after: The group resumes from nearby state and can demonstrate the corrected timing.
feedback: Health or rupee change, respawn or repositioning, peer movement, and repeated mechanism response show the correction path.
consequences: A weaker player can learn through continued participation while stronger players retain incentives in score performance.
observationThemes.factualDescription: Stage play supports repeated local combat and puzzle attempts within a continuous linked session, while collection totals provide a visible cost to inefficient play.
observationThemes.interpretation: Multiplayer recovery should preserve the gathering and connection cost while attaching failure to a legible, local resource consequence.

contributionHypotheses: A shared-clearance goal plus individual collection score can create productive cooperation-competition tension inside the same stage.
Private centered views can support parallel search if topology periodically requires explicit reunion.
Temporary per-player tool limits may turn inventory selection into negotiated team roles.
uniqueOrNonportable: The exact fourfold hero premise, named sword, villain, princess rescue frame, themed areas, keys, and rupee presentation are recognizable authored surface content and must not be reused.
Specific room layouts, boss sequences, player-carry animations, item roster, scoring values, and audiovisual identity are nonportable.
The required GBA multi-cartridge and cable arrangement is a historical product constraint, not a default recommendation for modern projects.
openQuestions: Original stage-layout variation and exact resource penalties require a primary-play trace before they support any deterministic generation claim.
The Anniversary Edition's single-player and wireless changes should be recorded as a separate variant delta, not blended into this profile.
